reviews.review_text:
[h1]See things as they are, not how you want them to be...[/h1]

The most beautiful game I have ever played, in every aspect! I think by now everyone has played or at least heard about Clair Obscure: Expedition 33, but I'm not going to spoil anything about the game in this review. The story is about a group of people living in an isolated town called Lumiere, inspired by France during the Belle Époque. A huge number can be seen on the monolith that the Paintress paints every year, which affects them, so they form a team of expeditioners to sail to her in hopes of killing her and stopping her from harming the people in Lumiere. This year, Expedition 33 is gathering to start the adventure. Will they be able to stop her and live life normally?
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3735188216

The gameplay is like that of old RPGs, where you explore a world map, travel from city to city, meet people, kill monsters, and upgrade your weapons. The fighting is turn-based, which I'm familiar with. Think of old Final Fantasy games when you play this game. It is clear it was inspired by those old games, and the atmosphere is similar to NieR, which is one of my favorite games. However, there is no manual save; the game autosaves regularly. Whenever you find a flag checkpoint, it will save automatically and let you fast-travel to another checkpoint. Make sure to touch the checkpoint flag whenever you want to save. 

The combat is turn-based with QTEs, which you can set to auto in the settings if that's not your thing. Each character has a different fighting style and set of skills. The only things I had to do repeatedly were parrying attacks and dodging. I found out that by learning and hearing the attack, it would be easy to parry it. It frustrated me a couple of times, especially with some bosses you can't kill unless you parry them, but you can always fail and learn how to do it next time. The game needs some grinding to kill some bosses. Try not to skip any monsters you see around; kill them to level up and earn new weapons.  
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3735506708

The artwork is outstanding, and every detail in the game is beautiful and meaningful. The color palette chosen for each area is mesmerizing, and the character's design is memorable. This game did something other games in my opinion failed to do. There is no UI and clutter while you are exploring the areas; you just walk around and enjoy each detail without having a health bar, a minimap, or a distracting list of objectives. You have the full screen to yourself and your character, which is a huge plus for me, as it lets me enjoy the environment.

The characters' animations and facial expressions are on point; you can see the emotions in their gazes, posture, and voices. The voice actors did an outstanding job capturing the essence of those characters. It felt like watching a movie; in each scene, you could see how they reacted, what they looked like, and how they received each piece of information. This alone makes you want to replay the game to watch those little details. The dialogues are extremely well written.   
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3735805052

Music is breathtakingly beautiful; each soundtrack is meaningful; the lyrics tell a story and capture the characters' emotions. The game has 154 tracks on the main album alone, worth listening to, and they will actually stick in your head after playing the game.    

Character development is one of the strong suits of this game; you can see and feel how they bond, how they become stronger, how they hate, and how they grow suspicious. The writing of each character is phenomenal, especially in Act 2.   

Pros:
+Emotional beautiful story.
+Outstanding soundtracks.
+Beautiful graphics and animations.
+Great characters and monsters design.
+Phenomenal voice over.
+Turn-based combat.
+World map.
+Fast traveling.
+Journal.
+Multiple endings.
+Collectables.
+Cosmatics for each character.
+Auto save.
+Steam achievements.

Cons:
- No manual save.


The game is unique in that it doesn't explain the story to you directly; it wants you to follow it emotionally and logically, forming theories about what is going on before the grand reveal. I highly recommend not charging and just killing bosses without exploring the world, reading the journals, or speaking to the characters. Those interactions will give you different layers to the story, and you'll understand it more, especially in the last act, where you'll be able to explore the whole map to read more information and learn more about the characters. The game has two endings; it is up to you how you want this to end. 


[quote]𝐹𝑜𝑟 𝑚𝑜𝑟𝑒 𝑟𝑒𝑣𝑖𝑒𝑤𝑠 𝑐𝘩𝑒𝑐𝑘 𝑚𝑦 𝑐𝑢𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑜𝑟 𝑝𝑎𝑔𝑒 [url=http://store.steampowered.com/curator/32399565/]𝐹𝑒𝑒𝑛𝑎'𝑠 𝐶𝘩𝑜𝑖𝑐𝑒[/url][/quote]
[quote][url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/32732116-IndieGems/] Follow [b]IndieGems[/b] [/url] for more reviews like this one. [/quote]

I bought this game to cope with the fact that I will lose a parent within the next couple of months. I needed a way to escape from reality momentarily, so nothing better than choosing a game with death, loss and grief as it's central themes, right? In the end, I have become addicted to it.

The story has been touching right from the start and I can easily say that it has one of the most outstanding intros to a game that I have got to experience in recent years. You get to care and almost cry for characters that you have only known for 30 minutes. The use of narrative elements is quite beautiful too and it often feels like watching a movie. Over the course of the story, you will get to know how other people in this world deal with grief; some manage to move on, others will be stuck in it forever. You won't be able to understand it until you have gone through it yourself and that aspect heavily influences your perception of the story.

The combat is extremely fun, yet challenging. Mastering timings and enemy patterns feels extremely rewarding and there's also a decent amount of end-game content to spend time with. The visuals are absolutely stunning and the music gave me back the passion for playing the piano that I have lost a few years ago. Definitely one of the best gaming OSTs out there. If there's one thing I disliked, it is the option to have some romantic dialogue with two of your companions. That felt very out of place considering the setting and several other factors. It is completely optional though and doesn't affect the story.

Genuinely one of the few games I have played that made me realize how beautiful life can be sometimes, even in times of hardship, if it means that I get to experience this work of art. It shows how much a small team can accomplish, as long as they show love and appreciation for their craft.
I was hesitant to play it, but after finishing it, it became one of the best games of my life. Thank you to the development team for creating this masterpiece.

TL;DR: Great visuals, music, and presentation, but beneath that I found a fairly standard JRPG with weak worldbuilding, uneven pacing, and combat systems that don’t fully work together. Not a bad game, but nowhere near the masterpiece its Steam rating suggests. If you are not already a JRPG fan, this will not change your mind. 

-----------

Man, I really wanted to like this game and leave a positive review, but after finishing it last week I just can’t. The game isn’t bad, but it is definitely overrated in my opinion. I don’t think it deserves the “Overwhelmingly Positive” rating on Steam, especially because games with that rating usually transcend their genre and can turn non-fans into fans. This game is not that. It’s a fairly standard JRPG with great graphics, music, and an interesting hook, but the gameplay and storytelling have enough issues that I don’t think it deserves all the praise it gets.

Most reviews praise the story and gameplay, but honestly I found those to be the weakest parts at times.

The biggest problem for me is the worldbuilding. The game throws you into this mysterious fantasy world but gives almost no grounding or context for a huge portion of the game. There’s little explanation for what kind of world this is, why everything is floating, who these creatures are, what the rules of the world are, or why things function the way they do. The core premise is interesting. A Paintress writes a number on a monolith and everyone of that age dies. But the game takes too long to give meaningful context to anything surrounding it.

Because of that, I struggled to become attached to the characters or invested in the stakes. I kept waiting for the game to explain what was actually happening and why. Even fantasy worlds need understandable rules. Instead, I often felt like I had missed important cutscenes or backstory. The Gestrals are a good example: the game clearly expects the player to love them, but there's no reason why. You have no idea what they even are. They mostly felt like Minions to me.

I had just finished Baldur's Gate 3 before playing this, and the difference in storytelling and worldbuilding was huge. BG3 constantly grounds the player in its world through factions, politics, lore, dialogue, and character motivations. My wife watched me play through most of BG3 and ended up starting up a character herself and putting 100 hours into that game. She never plays RPGs. Meanwhile E33 put her to sleep every time. E33 seems more interested in preserving mystery and emotional symbolism than building a believable world with understandable rules. I know that approach works for some people, but for me it made the story harder to connect with because I never fully understood the world or what was truly at stake.

Act 3 finally explains why the story was so mysterious, but by that point the game is pretty much over and the structure of the world starts causing gameplay problems. The world opens up and you’re encouraged to explore and do side content...or end the game. Except many of the new areas can instantly destroy you with little warning. Exploration became frustrating in Act 3 because progression is now based on trial and error rather than natural discovery. I got bored and wanted the game to end. I played other games. Eventually I returned, completed more side content, avoided much of the optional areas, became much stronger, and then ended up massively over-levelled for the final boss, which made the ending anticlimactic. Overall, Act 3 felt very rushed and really hurt the overall experience for me. 

The combat is also a mix of really good ideas and frustrating design decisions. The Pictos system is genuinely cool because it lets you create strong synergies and customize builds in interesting ways. Weapons are also varied enough to feel meaningful, especially when combined with certain Pictos. I also liked the upgrade system, which feels somewhat similar to Dark Souls.

That said, I think the attribute/stat system was a mistake. It was difficult to tell how much impact individual stats were actually having on combat, and by the end I mostly just maxed 1–2 core attributes and relied almost entirely on mastering dodge/parry timing. Since the game is so heavily built around reaction-based defense mechanics, stats often felt secondary or even pointless. Recoats are also so plentiful that respecing has very little consequence anyway.

To me, the game feels caught between two combat philosophies. Either the attribute system needed to matter much more, or the dodge/parry mechanics needed to be less dominant and punishing. As it stands, timing is far more important than character building, which made a lot of the RPG systems feel less meaningful than they initially appeared.

In the end, I don’t think it’s a bad game. It has excellent presentation and some genuinely interesting ideas. But for me, the uneven pacing, weak worldbuilding, and inconsistent gameplay balance keep it far from being a masterpiece. Most of the positive reviews I read of this game don't mention any of this so this is why I'm leaving a negative review.
Was not expecting timing/precision based combat mechanic in a turn based game. Regular mobs are able to kill my char in 2 hits on story difficulty. Im getting too old for this ♥♥♥♥.

This game is phenomenal. It turns out that when you let talented developers escape the Ubisoft corporate prison and you just let them cook, you get an actual masterpiece. The art style is breath-taking with incredibly smooth animations, and the story keeps you hooked from the very first minute. The soundtrack is an absolute god-tier masterpiece—it literally gives you chills and fits the whole atmosphere perfectly. Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is an absolute banger and Sandfall Interactive completely nailed it.

This game is a masterpiece. 

I have been on Steam for 16 years and I have never written a review, but I feel compelled to say SOMETHING about this game. It just excels everywhere: story, gameplay, level design, music, art, sound design, graphics... on every single metric, this game is outstanding. There is no room for improvement.

Back in the early 2000s, people used to debate about whether video games can be art. This game is art. It is a piece of art that will make you think and feel things, all while being one of the most fun experiences you will have in a modern game. Show anyone this game, and you are showing them a masterpiece.

I don't like to throw around phrases like "best game ever made" or "one of the best games ever," because you're comparing apples and oranges when it comes to different games (like, how could I possibly compare this with a game like The Last Of Us? It wouldn't make sense). Instead, I like to say there is just a "top tier" certain games have reached; they're flawless, memorable, replayable, discussable. Clair Obscur is a top tier game. It stands on its own alongside the rest of the best games you've ever played. 10/10 perfect, no room for improvement.

This is a neat game, and you can find a lot of praise and compliments. I also had fun with it, but in this review, I would like to focus on criticism because I hope somebody in the studio reads the feedback, and I’m looking forward to what comes next.

The mix between TB and RT in combat mostly works, but there are certain hiccups like not-so-well telegraphed attacks here and there, or infuriating mechanics like Vanish
The map doesn’t have player-made markers, and I had to keep notes in a notebook
The same with quests, there is no journal
With so many build options, it would be nice to have presets of weapons / luminas - manually selecting each thing when you want to experiment is tedious
At the end of Act 2, the story takes a turn for the worse and leads to an anticlimactic ending, and the amazing setup from the beginning of the game is ruined
Steam Deck “verified” is only on paper; the game looks and runs poorly on the device

Highly subjective and unpopular opinion, but the OST with constant operatic singing is tiresome.

Still, I will definitely get back for a New Game+ sometime later.

I HATE THIS GAME.

I hate the extremely cringy character animations. Whenever the characters aren’t walking around with completely dead expressions, they’re constantly doing these weird flirty smiles, slight shaking their heads and shrugging, and giving people that sideways look no matter who they’re talking to. I have never disrespected a game’s cutscenes this much. I literally started scrolling on my phone just to avoid looking at them while still trying not to miss important information by listening.

I hate the map design. Exploring this insanely huge and empty world for collectibles was just NOT FUN. Sure, the art direction looks impressive when you first enter a new area, but after a while it becomes exhausting to navigate all these confusing paths. You never know which path actually leads to the main quest, and as someone who loves exploration, it was incredibly annoying to still be trying to collect everything only to accidentally trigger a boss fight. You constantly run into invisible walls that look like you should obviously be able to cross, except OCCASIONALLY one random cliff actually leads to a secret path. Eventually I completely stopped caring about exploration because you almost never find anything interesting anyway. By the end, all I wanted was to finish the main quest and escape this confusing map.

I hate the UI/UX design. The character menus were so overwhelming that for the first 10 hours I completely gave up trying to understand how to build proper combat setups. I genuinely ended up fighting with random skills and brute-forcing every encounter by dying over and over until I memorized the enemies’ attack patterns, which also was not fun at all.

I hate the relationship system so much. The conversations throughout the game were already bland and unengaging, but every time you returned to camp, the game forced you to sit through five boring conversations in a row. And you don’t even dare skip them because they’re tied to unlocking combat skills. That is NEXT LEVEL torture. I honestly cannot believe anyone considers this fun, even hardcore RPG players.

The only reason I managed to play for more than 20 hours was because I wanted to respect the money I spent on it. But I’ve decided to let it go now.

Totally overrated.

I really wanted to enjoy this game because I watched streamers play it and I'm 1.5 hours into the cutscenes on YouTube. But unfortunately actually playing the game I got motion sickness. The view is often at diagonal angles. I also just feel like there's way too much going on in the screen with constant menus, zoom ins, zoom outs, attacks.

Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is a game about being stuck but unaware of it. It is a hollow spectacle, mechanically satisfying and with striking music and visuals, but it's let down by weak writing, by a disconnected mess of a third act, and by its strong points' repetitiveness. But what's worst is how [i]frustratingly close[/i] it keeps coming to being coherent and meaningful, to having [i]interesting things to say[/i].

The game's themes are creativity and grief and trauma, and losing oneself in any of these, and that [i]kind of[/i] fits with its art, where you can see stunning vistas while standing upon literal mountains of corpses, or hear beautiful music that conveys the same mood of tragedy and lament that all the music before it conveyed as well. These [i]could[/i] be made to work if the story [i]addressed[/i] these themes, but it refuses to.

A secondary theme is different forms of art or expression and the tension between them, but it feels like the game keeps forgetting about this right after it comes up, every time.

There is no character development happening either, at any point, despite ample opportunity for it. Nobody learns anything or grows in any way. Instead, there are interactions that check off the trope checkboxes and that make numbers go up.

Apart from the themes the characters' viewpoints are implicitly militaristic and colonialist, but even given that the game is surprisingly tone-deaf at times. Imagine if your Blue Mage's ability required them committing war crimes, and the game played that fact [i]for laughs[/i].

Mechanically Clair Obscur is a competent JRPG-with-lots-of-Quick-Time-Events with minor implementation problems, and if you enjoy those you will probably like this one as well, but it assumes familiarity with a lot of genre tropes and does a terrible job of explaining its own mechanics or even menus.

I am not into turn based games at all, I actively avoid them,
but when Baldurs Gate 3 released I gave it a go and it was very good.

Clair Obscur Expedition 33, is at the same level in a different way. The first several hours I played I didn’t really get what all the hype was about. 

Some part of me will always feel that because I don’t connect with these types of turn based games the way some others do, but I will say now that I’ve almost beaten it,  it’s a truly remarkable experience. 

This game is simply different, each element in isolation is familiar and done very well but combined in a way I haven’t seen before. It’s a game that continuously gets better and better as you progress through the world, and for the longest time nothing makes sense, but when things click it’s extremely rewarding and very well done.

Tldr, even if it isn’t your play style, it’s worth your time. It’s a true work of art.

I cannot put into words how fantastic this game is. From the very first minute, it completely captivated me—the music, the story, the animation, the attention to detail. I absolutely love this game and highly recommend it. It is a clear 10/10 and truly deserves the awards it has won.
I normally hate turn based games, but sure enough, the game of the year winner won me over too.

Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 

may be a little excessive at times — overly dramatic, overly emotional, and sometimes even overwhelming in the way it tells its story. But what I truly felt while playing this game was the love, passion, and dedication of the developers behind it.

This is a game that feels like it was created with genuine care and artistic passion, not just for the sake of making another product.

What stayed with me the most was how the game constantly made me question things: identity, acceptance, grief, and the difficulty of facing reality. It made me reflect on myself in ways I didn’t expect.

And honestly, that is the true charm of this game.

✩✩✩✩✩

A gorgeously presented, stylishly composed, and mostly very fun game (provided you don't mind parry mechanics) with a story that is excellent for the first act, and still pretty good from the second act until the ending. You may or may not enjoy the big twist, but regardless of narrative nitpicks, it is a fantastic game and a love letter to classic JRPGs, so if you are a fan of the genre, I'd say give it a shot!

Simply one of the best single player games I have ever played! The story had me hooked to the point that I put down all other games to keep playing this. I am actually sad that I finished it now because of how good the overall game was. I have a hard time sticking to games due to my ADHD brain just wandering off but this game had its hooks in me!

Just a beautiful game..
It's so rare we get games like this. 
I found myself finishing the game at 39 hours of gameplay. Normally I'd go to my last save before finishing and try to clean everything out, but not here. I made my choices, was happy with them, and felt fulffiled when the credits rolled. Amazing job, chef's kiss!

Guys I tried… if you are not loving this game don’t worry you are not the only one. I think the reason this game swept the game awards says a lot more about the lack of creativity in the gaming industry. E33 is new, creative, beautiful and really well made. It stands out in the state of the uninspired gaming industry, that however is not reason in itself to like the game. 

There are a lot of creative choices made in the making of this game and I love the effort and originality of it all, but it just simply didn’t land for me. And while I can write probably 4-5 pages in why I am going to keep it to 3 simple points. 

#1 story
I don’t like it. Intro was cool but it was pretty much the only time I enjoyed it. 30 hours in the games writing is very thin. There are lots of small moments that are meant to feel big that don’t give a lot of context. Something that I’m sure makes more sense if I complete the game, but right now it’s just a bunch of random things that don’t make sense yet. I’m clearly aware this is a storytelling style and it will all make sense at some point, but there just isn’t enough there that makes sense now (30 hours in) that would compel me to keep playing to find out. TLDR, I am bouncing off the story. 

#2 characters 
While this game is impressive in a lot of ways, the characters seem like they are hyper aware of the tropes they are trying to fit in. There are side characters who are made with self aware millennial humor that just straight up missed me. Perfect example of this is the stupid thing you fly on, he talks slow and is obviously meant to be a very endearing member of the party but does very little to earn that role. He’s simply kinda wacky and talks funny and you’re supposed to Looooove him and he’s probably a lot of people’s faaaaaaavorite. He just didn’t do it for me, I get that he plays the comic relief that brings some levity to the game, and if that works for you then that’s cool, just didn’t land for me. 

#3 combat 
Skill issue skill issue skill issue… yeah… the game is hard. I have beat Elden Ring, Nier Automata, Witcher 3 on Deathmarch. It’s not like I’m amazing at games but I’m not bad, but the parry system is just bad. The windows are super small and inconsistent, and I KNOW some people can do it so I’m honestly a little confused why I am having such a hard time with it. Sometimes I do it great but other times I just get wrecked and miss everything, maybe it’s a latency issue? Idk tbh, but the game punishes the hell out of your for getting hit, like my characters just die insanely fast when I don’t do everything perfectly that it makes me just want to turn it off when I get wrecked by some new enemy that has like 3 turns before I get 1. And being that the characters and story aren’t doing it for me, I just don’t have enough willpower to keep pushing through and I’m not interesting in lowering the difficulty because what am I even doing if I don’t care about the story and the game is easy and I’m not enjoying any of it? 

Anyway, I really hope I come back and give it a try sometime in the future. And to be clear, I’m not saying it’s objectively so horrible game, you may enjoy it, clearly a lot of people do, but let’s just not pretend it’s a universally amazing game. It’s really not for everyone and if it’s not for you, you aren’t alone. I’d give it like a 6/10.

[Review without any major spoilers]

I skipped over this game when it came out last year because I felt it was just another game. Then my friends and family played it and told me I HAD to play it because I really like RPGs. I was still "too busy" playing online games like League of Legends or War Thunder. 

This year the Clair Obscur concert was announced to come to our city and I heard about how amazing the music was and bought tickets right away without even having played a second.
8 days ago I finally booted it just 10 days before the concert (yes I played 43 hours in a week, the main story + every side zone that was doable before postgame for a rough estimation of how long this game is).

Ever since the release I knew I would play eventually so I stayed away from any spoilers (somehow managed to avoid knowing almost anything except like the whole concept of gommage and seeing a picture of Esquie)

So I started and instantly was mesmerized by the main title theme. I spent a good 15 minutes there before clicking play.

Then started and grew very attached to the characters, even more the ones waiting for their gommage.
Then grew confused why there was this dude on the beach, but was so invested in the next scene that I kinda forgot about him until he showed up again.

And that's something amazing about this game. You have all these questions that need answering so much that you forget to ask broader questions like "what is this world ? what is the Fracture ?" and later when you "learn" that [spoiler] Renoir and Verso were made immortal by the paintress, I was wondering "who else from the expeditions is immortal ?" not "why them ?" which is the more valid question regarding what comes after. [/spoiler]
Absolutely brilliant world building (heh) and story telling. 

There's also this absolutely incredible musical score that sometimes makes you stop playing just to listen, like when the characters were mesmerized by Siren's singing, I was too.

The end was breathtaking too, terrible choice for the characters who I felt so much with.

As of today I haven't played much of the post-game but I will definetly. I don't want to leave this world (wink wink). The whole theme really resonates with me.

Today I kinda regret not living the hype of the release with others, but I still got to share this experience and I'm forever grateful I did. Now, like the characters, I have to grieve. Grieve this time of my life when I discovered this world for the first time, because it's not coming back.

Merci Sandfall


[Specs side note]

I played with a pretty old graphics card and had to spend 2 hours to optimize the game and make it able to run on my GTX 1050 Ti at 30 FPS, would not recommend.

If I were given 100 more lives, I would choose to play this game 101 times.

If someone asked me to pick the greatest game ever made, I wouldn’t hesitate for a second — this would be my choice.

This is not just a game to me; it’s an experience that left a mark on me long after I put down the controller.

Few stories make you laugh, suffer, hope, and grieve alongside their characters. This one did.

Every hour I spent in its world felt worthwhile, and every moment of its journey reminded me why I fell in love with video games in the first place.

If one day I lost all memory of it, I would gladly experience it again from the beginning, just to feel those emotions for the first time once more.

I don't really know how to describe this game. It's really good—the story, the battles, the characters. Everything feels alive. I enjoyed every single moment of this game. You'll need to learn how to parry to really enjoy it, but it's worth it.

Give it a try, I believe you'll love it.

A 100% well-deserved Game of the Year. Before I decided to try it, I was very skeptical, but that changed every time someone around me started or finished playing it, and all the reviews were rave. Once I started playing, the game drew me in from the prologue, and even then I understood why it was so highly praised. From the gameplay to the visuals and storyline, the game is wonderful. I highly recommend it, even to newcomers to the genre, which I, to some extent, was.

After 112 hours within the expanse of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, I can confidently say that this is one of the most breathtaking and enjoyable games I’ve ever played. From the moment the title screen appears, a piece of music plays so divine it might as well have been composed by angels on a lunch break in Paris, even I had to stand there in shock wondering whether this was merely a game or a ticket to the French opera house lol.

Expedition 33 is a turn-based RPG that gleefully raids the larders of the genre’s greatest hits to construct a dense, interconnected web of mechanics. It evokes Persona with its stylish, freely aimed projectiles and audacious battle presentation. It borrows the spirit of Final Fantasy IX through equippable items called Pictos, which allow party members to learn abilities in a way that feels both nostalgic and dangerously addictive. Timed button hits inject a surprising layer of immersion into every attack, to the point where you briefly begin to believe you could recreate the exact same combo in real life if someone just gave you a sufficiently dramatic menu screen and a small amount of confidence XD. There’s even a hint of Soulslike DNA in how weapon scaling and stat relationships demand respect, patience, and the occasional existential crisis! ;) 

Yet despite all these influences, the game never collapses into imitation. It confidently stands as its own creation—so much so that I’ve said it before, but it feels especially true here: Clair Obscur plays like many games, but nothing else plays like Clair Obscur.

The biggest differentiator is the sheer intensity of battle. This is not your friendly neighbourhood turn-based system where you sip tea and casually select “attack.” No—this is a full-contact intellectual sport where hesitation is basically an invitation to be turned into decorative battlefield mulch. Dodging and parrying are not optional flourishes; they are survival requirements. And for a perfectionist like me, that means you end up memorising attack patterns with the dedication of someone studying for an exam they didn’t sign up for, as enemies attempt to turn your expedition into something resembling sashimi lmao.

Every party member brings a distinct mechanical identity that turns combat into a layered orchestration of systems. Gustave builds charges with every hit before unleashing devastating Overcharge attacks like a man slowly winding up emotional damage. Lune manipulates elemental stains to escalate her spells into increasingly catastrophic outcomes, essentially turning chemistry into a war crime. Maelle, meanwhile, dances between stances like a theatrical executioner, capable of absurd damage output under the right conditions—or absolutely nothing if the universe decides to humble you today.

Their skill trees remain deliberately concise, which keeps the system approachable without stripping away depth. Instead of drowning you in options, the game asks you to understand the few you do have, then weaponise them with frightening efficiency. The result is a combat system that feels less like menu navigation and more like learning how to conduct controlled chaos.

Then there are the Pictos—small items with an unreasonable amount of influence over reality. At first they seem manageable. Then suddenly you are 30 menus deep, questioning your life choices while trying to remember whether “+AP on perfect dodge” stacks with “AP refund on emotional damage,” lol. Once mastered, however, they unlock build diversity that feels genuinely transformative, allowing you to shape characters in ways the base skill trees alone simply cannot support.

Early on, I genuinely thought the game might be too easy. I was comfortably steamrolling most encounters in the first third of the journey, occasionally stumbling into optional bosses who took no hesitation in clobbering my expedition into chroma paste! XD Then the difficulty curve tightened its grip. Enemy patterns grew more elaborate, systems began interlocking in increasingly demanding ways, and suddenly every fight felt like a rhythm game designed by someone who thought of blinking as a mere suggestion lol.

And yet, it never becomes overwhelming for the sake of it. It stays focused. Controlled. Deliberate. Even when it pushes complexity, it does so with intent, allowing skilled players to “break” the system in rewarding ways rather than exploitative ones. It’s the kind of design that makes mastery feel earned rather than cheesed, even when you're facing a balding man with a flimsy paintbrush and a tendency to spontaneously transform into the epitome of a humanized helicopter! XD

But underneath all of this mechanical brilliance lies a story steeped in inevitability. In Lumière, almost no one lives untouched by death. It is not an abstract fear—it is a scheduling conflict with mortality. Orphanages overflow, families debate whether bringing children into the world is hope or cruelty, and society fractures between those who accept decline and those who dedicate themselves to resisting it. Some live quietly with their remaining time, while others pour themselves into research, art, or preparation for the annual expeditions sent to confront the Paintress—the mysterious force erasing humanity piece by piece.

The success rate of these expeditions? Effectively zero. Which is, admittedly, not the most encouraging job advertisement in history lol. And yet, for those with only a year left to live, joining Expedition 33 becomes less of a heroic choice and more of a final act of defiance. If everything else has failed, then failure itself becomes the stepping stones to build off.

At its core, the narrative is about grief, legacy, and the strange ways humans choose to continue living in the shadow of inevitable loss. It explores family—not just the one you are born into, but the one you build along the way—and how art, memory, and shared experience become tools for processing the unthinkable. This is carried by a deeply human cast: Sciel’s infectious optimism cutting through despair like sunlight through fog, and Lune’s relentless curiosity transforming uncertainty into purpose. Around campfires and quiet conversations, they slowly become more than party members—they become fragments of hope stitched together under impossible circumstances. And then, after all the systems, battles, and emotional weight… there’s a rare kind of magic that lingers.

There’s a feeling that Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is not just a complete experience, but the beginning of something larger—a foundation for something timeless. A game that makes you want not just to finish it, but to understand every corner of it, every system, every story thread, every inch of its world. By the time the credits rolled, I didn’t just want a sequel. I wanted a whole series, of course subsequently fit with all the ptsd I was put through in the original game on expert difficulty lmao.

I highly recommend Expedition 33, and it will forevermore stand in the annals of the best games I’ve ever had the pleasure to immerse myself in. For those who come after…
Art. One of the best games I've ever played. I got so lucky I didn't get anything spoiled for me and went in completely blind. Genuinely it's top 5 of all time.

This was a fantastic game with a great story and incredible music. The combat was fun and the enemies were unique. It is easily in the top 10 list of games I have played, and I highly recommend checking it out.

One of the best stories I've experienced in gaming, I was very hooked from beginning to end, the twists and conclusions all felt satisfying, and all the characters were likeable and had amazing dialogue.
The gameplay was fun and introduces a parry and dodge mechanic to a turn-based combat which I thought was very engaging, it gets a little grindy near the end but that's fine.

I also loved the soundtrack which made the story cutscenes and bossfights that much better.

Legend of Dragoon people, you will love this game. It's the exact same mechanics, style, story layout etc. of a classic game I love.  Finally finding a game that implements good solid mechanics the same way is amazing.  

If anyone from Sandfall ever sees this - get the copyright to Legend of Dragoon and remaster it PLEASE!

Don't have a single bad thing to say abou the game. Fun, and the music is amazing. The game is living proof that big budget studios over-complicate video game making. Fire execs, let the devs run the show.
You already know the story. AAA corps and yes men hated the idea of a startup company face rolling them at game awards far and wide...but despite all their rage, Sandfall still did it.

For those who come after: the answer is yes, some games can indeed generate an insane amount of hype and still impress in every possible way, and this is one of them. All the praise and awards it got are well deserved, in fact I would have given a couple more to Lorien Testard for his amazing soundtrack.

Good luck with those minigames though.

Absolute masterpiece, Expedition 33 is truly the greatest game I've ever played, blending a powerful, emotional, story with incredible music and engaging turn based combat, it deserves its praise. But the game's story is where it's truly outstanding. This game has a story on a completely different level to any game I've previously played, and even better than some stand alone books in movies. I can't stress more how much I love this game.

This game revolutionized turn-based combat by giving you something to do during your opponent's turn. It seems obvious, but this one change along with killer music and a heart-wrenching story makes this a masterpiece. You need to get this amazing game.

I really LOVE this game. You guys should definitely try it, 10/10. Buying this game was honestly one of the BEST decisions i’ve made.
This is my first turn based game and i didn’t expect to enjoy it this much. I love the mechanics, the combat feels really FUN, and the MUSIC is so fkn GOOODDDDD. Everything just feels SATISFYING to play.
ABSOLUTELY WORTH IT.
it is totally game of the year. If you will play on it , you will have  a things that you want from a game - story , feelings....recomended

I honestly didn’t expect Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 to hit me the way it did. I went into it thinking it would just be another decent story game, but by the end I just sat there staring at the credits for a while trying to process everything.

The story is easily one of the most emotional rollercoasters I’ve played through in years. The characters feel real, not perfect fantasy heroes, but people dealing with grief, fear, hope, and trying to keep moving forward. Gustave was definitely my favorite. Something about his character just felt genuine throughout. Sciel being my favorite for combat situations. Nothing lame about badass scythe using.

What surprised me most is that I normally do not like turn-based combat at all, but this game completely changed my mind on it. The boss fights were actually fun and satisfying instead of feeling slow or repetitive. Even on Story difficulty there were moments that still challenged me enough to stay engaged. At time I could only imagine what hard difficulty would do to my soul. There being no achievement for difficulty had an impact on me not playing on hard.

The soundtrack is unbelievable. There were multiple moments where the music alone carried the emotion of a scene before anyone even spoke. Combined with the art direction and world design, the whole game feels beautiful almost the entire way through. At times I would stream in discord showing my buddies the world art with the music and just jam out. The facial animations and expressions during cutscenes are honestly some of the best I’ve seen. Some scenes felt less like watching a game and more like watching actual people going through something painful. I know I did on multiple scenes.

I’ve seen some people complain that exploration is too narrow, but honestly I liked that. Huge open-world games usually burn me out because I get distracted doing random stuff for many hours and forget the main story exists. Expedition 33 kept me focused, and I think the game is better because of it.

My only real complaint was the world traversal at first. Early on I thought traveling around was going to become a massive pain, but once the story progresses further into endgame it really stops being an issue.

Replayability is probably the weakest part for me. I’d recommend doing all the side content the first time through because I personally don’t feel much reason to replay the entire game or do the optional New Game Plus. It’s a very focused and linear experience with little player choice, but honestly I think that works in its favor. 

This game genuinely surprised me. It made me emotional, made me care about the characters, and somehow made me enjoy a genre I usually avoid. That alone says a lot.

I would recommend this game to just about anyone, that is saying quite a lot with my picky personality. Game runs very great I was maxed out on all settings with 5070ti and 7800x3D never has any performance issues, one freeze during combat that’s it. I do plan on returning and finishing out the rest of the optional content and achievements sometime in the future, but I got what I came for and had a blast doing just story content.
Great Game, Well Done 👍
continues the jrpg tradition of having perhaps the worst mini games of all time. This game is solid but not close to the masterpiece people want to pretend it is.

I picked up this game thinking that there's no way it could live up to all the acclaim and the hype, but wanted to see what all the fuss is about. The gameplay, the combat mechanics, the story... 

I've experienced plenty of loss in my life. This game asks questions and handles topics that hit me deeper than I could ever have expected. My only problem with it is I can't experience it all again for the first time.

You owe it to yourself to experience this.

[h3]I didn’t finish the game. The game finished me.[/h3]

Just beat it and my heart was NOT ready for that ending. F*** the haters, this game’s an actual masterpiece and that is a hill I will die on. Expedition 33 truly deserves to be GOTY and every award it’s been getting.

Tbh, I bought the game because the visuals looked insane, but I ended up getting way more than I expected. The story was beautiful and bittersweet right from the start. Had a crybaby like me tearing up multiple times. Ngl, the big reveal [spoiler]by the end of Act 2[/spoiler] threw me off at first, but it didn’t ruin the game for me at all.

Gameplay-wise, I’m still pretty new to JRPGs, so this game taught me a lot. Optimizing builds, adjusting setups depending on enemies, actually learning dodge/parry timings. [b]I just wish the game taught me how to parry emotional damage too.[/b]

And the soundtrack was absolute fire!!! Fighting bosses while bangers like Une vie à t'aimer play in the background felt so good. Peak gaming experience! The characters were so memorable too, especially fluffy mon ami Esquie. A lot of the camp interactions and cinematic cutscenes felt so meaningful, they really got me so attached (and emotionally devastated).

Got pretty lucky playing during the 1-year anniversary too because the extra content and collectibles made the experience even better. So, thank you, devs!

Still recovering from the ending rn. My curious and slightly sadistic self decided to try both endings so yeah, I’m cooked. Anyway, I’m still probably gonna try to 100% this game because I'm a suffering-enjoyer lol

Bought this on a whim. Didn't know much about it and normally this isn't the type of game I really enjoy, but man am I glad I took the chance. It is one of the best games I've played in a long time. The story is fantastic, the combat is fun, the characters and humor are delightful. If you're on the fence, do it.

Wonderful story, GREAT combat with many different options and playstyles to go for per character. Definitely recommend!

And the music... Anyone telling you to turn down the music volume needs to be told to shut up!

Great game, (10/10) 

First game that i played in long time that made me want to play it more and more and more and more :D
thats why i did all achievements.

One of the greatest story i´ve ever seen (hopefully a film adaptation will be good (if it will come out))

since i did not played that many of turn based games i can´t say much about that but i enjoyed it very much. i can imagine that without QTE it would not be that engaging.

And the best part is that you cannot overlevel while you are in the middle of the story. So all main bosses feel hard and need to be beat clasic way and its chalanging and rewarding. After that yea go for it.
but I can tell you there are special bosses that you can fight even at the highest level that wont feel easy.

In my opinion this is must play at least once in lifetime.

Holy Peak!!!

Never have I ever thought that a game can be that good. For me, Expedition 33 stands alongside Elden Ring.

The soundtrack alone would justify the full price of admission, yet somehow it is only one part of an absolute masterpiece. I almost feel like I should have paid more.

The story is phenomenal. The soundtrack is a 33333333/10. The gameplay delivers some of the most enjoyable turn-based combat I have ever experienced, elevated even further by one of the greatest mechanics in modern gaming: parrying.

The only real downside is that this game may ruin every other game you play afterward.

Thank you to Sandfall Interactive, Kepler Interactive, Lorien Testard, and everyone involved in creating this unforgettable experience.

Absolute Wheeeee.
It's an excellent game, and it's replayability is great too. I played it last year on launch and I picked it up again a year later. It's just as captivating the second time. 👍

This game breaks the rating scale for me. When I rated other games as 10/10, I now have to consider them more like 10/11, with Expedition 33 being an 11/11.

The acting, writing, and soundtrack are the best I have ever experienced — including in the movie industry. I have never felt more immersed, emotionally invested, or heartbroken.

The only downsides I found with the game were that it was a bit hard to get into in terms of equipment and how Lumina works, but once you get over that, it never becomes a problem again.

The second thing was that the pacing of Act III didn’t match the first two. This especially applies to the insane jump in difficulty, which forced me to lower the difficulty setting — something I’m still a bit salty about.

All in all, I would still highly recommend this game.
Honestly one of the best games I've ever played. Do your best to not look up too many things about it. Just play and get lost in the story.

I honestly enjoyed this one way more than Baldur's Gate 3. The story and gameplay is genuinely good so far as I just finished Act 1. Looking forward to the next Acts.

One of the best RPGs ever made.

Cannot recommend enough!
I really don't have words. There is so much to love in this game, I don't know where to start. 
The characters feel very complex and real, and it's so easy to get attached to many of them... in honesty, more than I thought I was signing up for and I'm not over it but I digress... The story is engaging and impactful and messy, driven by the strengths and flaws of the characters. The music covers so many genres and there are so many standouts from the soundtrack. The art is colourful, rich, stylized, and unique. Don't get me started on character design and in-game character styling!
I found the combat challenging enough to keep me engaged but not overwhelming or unreasonable. Late game build creation is very satisfying for the bigger challenges in the game. This said, you can modify the game's difficulty to what you prefer.
The worldbuilding is so interesting, and I found myself scouring the environments and texts for lore. And I mean it when I say this, it's everywhere.
Incredible game, and I can't wait to see what the studio does next!

Don't be like me. Whatever you might have assumed about this game, disregard it. This is not another generic RPG relying entirely on fancy graphics or over-complicated menu systems. This isn't a souls-like that'll have you yeeting your controller through a concrete pillar because you were stomped yet again by the tutorial boss and can't seem to "get gud". This is a great game, a unique and memorable game, and a game well worth playing. 

I initially avoided this game thinking it was yet another FF-like, and by that I'm referring to their newer stuff with flat, boring AF characters. Yes, it looked pretty, just as FF games do, but at that price tag, I wasn't entirely eager to give it a shot and risk enduring characters I abhored ("Never fear, Yuna's here!"). 

However, like many games, I had made a snap judgment and wrote it off... only to try it later and discover I was an absolute idiot for sleeping on this.  

For full disclosure, I haven't yet beaten the game, and have no idea how far along I am (halfway? maybe?) -- however, the more I play the more invested I become. Yes, the game is incredibly beautiful, and the music is *perfect*... but more than that, I like the characters! They aren't leaning cartoonishly into tropes nor slamming their quirks in your face; they are nuanced and interesting and feel *real*. The world is gorgeous, fascinating, unsettling and NEW. The villain, as enigmatic as she still remains at this point in this story, remains an ever-present and intriguing force. You've got twists, you've got a fun-yet-challenging combat system (deserving a paragraph of praise for that feat alone), and you've got lots and lots of optional content to explore. There are cute moments, funny moments, dark moments, and heavy moments. More than anything, there's a lot of WOW moments. 

There's a reason the reviewers are singing such high praises for this game, and why it has won those awards. It's worth buying, it's worth playing, it's worth your time and money. I'm only halfway(?) through, but this game has left such an impression. Don't be an idiot like me... go buy play this game before you stumble on spoilers and wish you'd had the chance to experience it yourself. You won't be disappointed!

Fantastic FF style game, 
Story, fights, leveling, just briliant
This is a game that you find quite things about yourself at the end. It reminds you that you have to do some hard choices for to do the right thing.

This is the type of game you play and wonder - why can't everything be this beautiful.
High recommend.

I recently finished playing Clair Obscur: Expedition 33. I have to say it's one of the best games I have ever played. Everything about the game is so well crafted, they all come together to give you this amazing experience. 

The narrative is a breath of fresh air. On the surface, it presents itself as a straightforward fantasy action-adventure, but as the story progresses, it gradually reveals itself to be something entirely different. What makes it especially compelling is its focused and tightly paced structure. The characters are also thoughtfully written and felt like real people.

Visually the game is what you would call an eye candy. The art direction for the world and charachter design is so good that it makes you want to keep exploring new areas and just keep looking at the landscape and characters. The animations was also done really well. Specially the facial animation, it felt almost... real. 

What can I even say about the music and soundtracks, they just elevate every other parts of the game to the next level. You can just listen and vibe to the music all day. [But don't go out of your way to translate the lyrics. they are filled with spoilers.]

Now, for the main part, the gameplay. I was skeptical about the whole turn based system. But I ended up enjoying it. The real time elements (free aim, parry, dodge), unique character mechanics, skills, buffs-debuffs, pictos and luminas made the combat fairly deep, but not deep that you would drown. 

Outside of combat, you can explore different areas of the map, encounter and deafeat various enemies and bosses, talk to quirky and enegmatic NPCs, play mini games, find collectibles and power ups, and learn more about the world and characters. 

In summary, Every aspect of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 felt like it was made with passion, care and thought. And it is an absolute amazing game to play through.
Generational game with probably the best soundtrack I’ve ever heard. I've had multiple dreams about this game and I finished it a month ago now.

I'm a musician and this game is every musician gamers' heaven. Guillaume Broche and Sandfall have so much respect from me for bringing Lorien Testard to do the soundtrack and also for him to bring Alice Duport-Percier and the Curieux orchestra to do the music. I do have some criticism regarding some mechanics (like NG+ doesn't feel too necessary to me, or the parry system being tough - but both are personal taste and it didn't bother me too much)

All in all, it's a really fun to play masterpiece of art that is made by people who poured love into their game. I really hope they explore the game's world in later potential expansions or standalone games.[spoiler]The world outside the painting, and the conflict between the Writers and Painters. [/spoiler]

Personally as a turn based RPG enjoyer I have to say that mixing real time dodge mechanics into a turn based game feels quite gross to me. Obviously I'm not the popular option by the sheer amount of positive reviews but if the idea of dodge mechanics in a turn based game disgusts you then this definitely isn't for you.

Very fun and engaging game, I keep coming back to it after I've done almost everything.  Parry and dodging are fun and satisfying.  Great story, great music, great game play - highly recommended.  I did buy it on sale and it was worth it; I believe it was during the Christmas or winter sale; if you are on the fence about getting it, then definitely pick it up during a sale.

Extraodinary game, music is out of this world, the voice actors killed it, exciting gameplay and a unique story that keeps you on your toes from start to end. My only complaint is that some of the areas are a little hard to explore without a map of some kind but overall this game deserves the praise

I got this game very skeptical of the absolute glaze fest the game was met with and what seemed like from an out of nowhere release. There are a few nitpicks here and there such as the very poor facial animations and what feels like mis-timed parry frames but aside from that the game is an amazing journey through an original story with a strong performance by the VAs and writing team. I'd suggest this to anyone who likes RPGs.
Wonderful story, music, graphics, and gameplay! One of my top games ever.

Took me on an emotionally rollercoaster
first turn based game I've actually really got into
and first game in a while that really made me want to do all the content it had to offer
10/10 would erase my memory to play this masterpiece again

Masterpiece, amazing story, great gameplay, spectacular visuals, fantastic music.

10/10.

Its a turn-based souls-type game. It lacks key elements you would find in an RPG such as an economy  (you can't sell items) or inventory management (all restorative items regenerate after resting). It has the illusion of being open world but its pretty linear where you can go until the end. You have quite a lot of options with character builds but you really only need glass cannons. Superbosses can be killed in one shot with the proper glass cannon and two support characters. It is a pretty easy game once you figure out the metas.

Sound: Eargasm
Combat: Amazing
Story: A bit confusing but ok, maybe too much cutscenes
Game performance could also be a bit better since I have high end PC and the game revolves around 60-70 FPS

Overall: Solid 8.5/10

This game made me cry like a baby. There are 2 endings for it and you get to choose the one you feel is right. It definitely deserves a try because the story is very unique and unforgettable. You can also explore the depth of the story when you roam over the map and find things around which is one of my fav things in games. The map is a bit different than most games though. The only thing that's not to my liking is the combat system (reactive turn-based),  but it is not so bad to the point of ruining your gaming experience. Overall, it deserved my time.....

This game has won a huge number of awards and rightfully so. Many elements of the game are absolutely exceptional. The music is phenomenal, the story is compelling and poignant, the combat is frequently challenging enough to be engaging but not so challenging as to become monotonous through repetition. I loved the characters, I loved the story, I loved the music - I loved the experience of the game. The fights required enough skill and timing that beating the enemies felt rewarding to beat and the scaling was pretty well done for the first two-thirds of the game. 

The game isn't perfected though - it has some issues with pacing in the latter stages as you become far too strong if you don't push straight through to the main quest ending after about two-thirds of the game. The variety of builds also allow for you to become ridiculously strong and effectively break the game (although this isn't uncommon in the JRPG style combat systems). There are a few mini-games and variety of optional fights with unusual or unintuitive mechanics or just awkward controls - these are mostly a nice change of pace but some of them can become frustrating. The developers also released some super late-game content and the ramp up to fight these compared to the final boss or other late-game content which existed before is absolutely massive. I assume this is to give players who want to spend a lot of time farming and finding broken builds something to do but I found it off-putting.

My gripes with the game are minor elements though and Clair Obscur deserved the game of the year award(s) it received and honestly it probably deserved a few more. I would recommend the game to anyone and if you don't like the game then you'll definitely like the music at least - 8.5/10.
Absolute cinema. Unbelievable soundtrack. Great gameplay. Really can't find anything negative to say about it. 10/10

For those who come after,

This isn’t just Game of the Year—it’s the Best Game of All Time. This game has even surpassed the Resident Evil series, which has been my favorite game series since I was a kid. This game also feels similar to Attack on Titan in some ways, which is my all-time favorite anime (of course I’m aware of the anime’s flaws, but emotionally, it’s still number one for me). It’s been a long time since I’ve felt this much excitement playing a single-player game. The storyline, themes, world-building, character design, art style, soundtrack, and gameplay mechanics all come together perfectly.

Since this game has soulslike elements, let me share my experience with the soulslike games I’ve played (Elden Ring, Dark Souls 3, Sekiro, Jedi). For the record, I don’t really care whether this game technically qualifies as a soulslike or not. If this game is fun for me, that’s what matters most.

I usually don’t enjoy soulslike games that much; if you want to call me weak, I don’t mind. If you want to know why, here’s the explanation: 

I’m not really that frustrated with the difficulty level, but for some reason, the rewards I get after beating a boss aren’t very satisfying, so I just don’t feel like playing anymore. Maybe I just don’t like the fact that I can’t fight the boss right away, but have to repeat the same path over and over. Repeating the same challenges I’ve already gone through is annoying. And also maybe I just don’t like grinding or mastering skills without anything new idea to discover. In this game, it’s not just about the curiosity of what kind of boss attack patterns I’ll face later on, but also about how the story will unfold, what beautiful places I’ll see, what wonderful soundtrack I’ll hear, and what funny things I’ll discover along the way.

For someone like me who’s grown tired of open-world games—because, with my perfectionist tendencies, I feel compelled to explore every single thing in these games, whose gameplay formulas haven’t changed much since I was a kid, and of course, that’s incredibly time-consuming. Even though I’ve tried to tone down my perfectionist tendencies, I still tend to get bored with open-world games—but that doesn’t apply to this one. In fact, I’m actually quite excited to explore everything. 

Of course, there are a few very minor bugs, but considering this game was made by a new indie studio—one that’s learning Unreal Engine for the first time while developing this as their debut title—it’s truly impressive.

Note: I first tried it on Xbox Game Pass until I finished Act 1. I was interested in buying the game, so I purchased it and then transferred my save file from there to here. That’s why my playtime is so short as I write this review.

I watched the GOTY Awards 2026 and saw that this game won half of them. I knew it existed but never bothered to give it a real look because I'm not a huge fan of turn based combat. I bought it afterwards and then left it for a few months until I finished my other games.

I don't think I have ever been this emotionally invested in a video game before. This was by far the best story telling I have seen and particularly if you like very abstract philosophical concepts.

The graphic, soundtrack and gameplay were also amazing. I did end up enjoying the combat a lot however I did put it on easy mode because my brain is normally to exhausted to care about builds and stuff at the end of the day.

One of the best games I have ever played hands down...

the most breathtaking game ive ever played till date. the graphics, the story, the soud design, the music, the dialogue are just top notch. you could easily make this a movie and get a billion dollars and the best picture award. no wonder this won the GOTY. i definitely recommend this game for anyone who is fan of deep story driven games and whoare into turn based strategy.

Simply buy this game and get out of the reviews! I cannot understate how amazing this experience was, and I would give anything to be able to experience it for the first time all over again. At this point I have over 100 hours in this game and have bought it 3 times over (2 copies for friends). My advice to new players: do not look anything up. You are not out of spoiler territory until the credits roll. This is a game that I cannot stop talking about to friends and had such a thought provoking story. BUY THIS GAME NOW YOU WILL THANK ME LATER

Excellent music, fantastic story and great entertainment.
I wouldn't call this classic turned based since it requires reacting. It would be better without the need to dodge or parry.
Beautifully made game. Both story and art direction is superb. Fun combat mechanics for a RPG.

Genuinely the greatest game of the decade. 100% deserved the awards sweep.
One of the only games I actually got emotional over the story in, and the world is fascinating as well as beautiful. Not to mention the music, which was insane in it's own right. The story is better than the majority of movies I've seen in the past few years, makes you love the characters, and makes you cry. If you can get this game, get it.

This is not just game, this is a masterpiece made out of pure joy and the most pure love that ever existed in this solar system. There is no experience such as this bunch of moving pixels and soundwaves that exhilirates this beauty, one of the videogames world wonders.

Since the beggining, I loved with all my hearth, and highly reccomend giving all your thoughts, feelings, hearth and brain to comprehend the complexity and beauty of Expedition 33.

I will be here, waiting for Expediton 60 and 0 games, and you should do the same, for those who come after!

This was my first turn based game and it was truly a masterpiece the painterly art style the music and the mind blowing story a truly amazing concept the emotionally connecting characters and at last a big choice this game is absolutely must experience try it you will never regret. 
Score 9.5/10
Great game, 30 hours into it and I'm still losing track of time whilst playing it.

Well, I finally got all achievements on Expedition 33!
Beautiful, beautiful game. I'm so glad I got to play it, even if it's turn-based (which I don't enjoy much).
Parry FTW!!!
It definitely deserves its GOTY Award, and I'd recommend it to anyone.
Incredibly cinematic, as everyone else says, but it's honestly true. The level of detail and care into mood, environment, and wonder, is simply phenomenal.

Really, really good game. Absolutely gorgeous. Role playing game elements are really well done, and if character and party tweaking is your interest, there's a lot in here to like. The story is out of this world. Maybe my only personal knock is that I gather multiple playthroughs are what's required to really unlock everything. With limited time, I'm not always a fan of multiple playthroughs. Some people seem to really enjoy that. I do wish the game would unlock 95% of the core mechanics and character moveset without having to rinse and repeat. All in all, absolutely gorgeous. One of the best games I've played in a long time. Very worth your time and money.

Hot damn, I think some devs had some pent up creativity and pumped it ALL into this game. This will probably be the most stunning and innovative game made this decade. The story, visuals, and gameplay are all incredibly novel. I was in awe all 30 hours, and deeply disturbed by the ending.

A MUST PLAY GOTY!!!

Sandfall Interactive & Kepler Interactive made an amazing, incredible, spectacular & special game!!
I laughed, cried & connected with the characters like I haven't in a game in years, if ever! it is 1 of my favorite games ever, if not my favorite!
If you like / love games with a good story, characters, gameplay & soundtrack, then I can't emphasize how much you need to play this game! You will love it! I know I do!
The Story is Beyond Words!
The Characters are Amazing!
The Gameplay / Combat is Smooth & Incredible!
The Soundtrack is Amazing Beyond Words!

This is my Game of the Year hands down! It will take an truly spectacular game to change my mind.

I won't put any spoilers into this review because if you're going to experience this game, I believe you need to go in blind. I only played about a year after it came out, only hearing about how great and wonderful it was. I will be adding to those great and wonderful reviews.

What I am going to recommend the most is the story. The graphics are stunning and out of this world. The music and sound is absolutely beautiful. But the story? A game has never made me laugh and cry as much as this one has. The characters are so well written and I truly fell in love with them. I wanted to know their stories and sit around the fire with them. I was so enthralled with the game that every time I played, it was truly like I was there.

The story brings so much more than I ever could have experienced. From the first act to the last, everything took me by surprise. I believe that once you start this game, no matter how long it takes you, you must finish it. The story and the emotions I experienced is so unlike anything I have experience before. That comes from someone who has read lots of books, played many a games, and watched many movies and TV shows. Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 is nothing short of amazing. It leaves behind a powerful message and there are so many surprises. This is a truly wonderful game.

For those that come after.
Amazing game with great story, likeable characters and lots of nice tracks in its OST

The game is very beautiful world build and really great story...also when the game start in menu the song are really mesmerizing and very beautiful...but its a shame that my laptop with 4gb vram most of the time it stutter and i dont mind during cutscene cuz i can still enjoy it but during parry on battle ground with the enemy its really hard cuz of the laggy sometimes...thank god i only play main story on easy so not too bad..if i play the side quest the simon boss i think it will be very hard to play againts it... thats all from me ty

Hands down the best game I've ever played, compelling story with gut wrenching, serious and amusing moments that are well placed to not hinder the narrative. The soundtrack is filled with mesmerising and beautiful songs, which the game takes advantages of to create gorgeous and engaging scenes.

If you have the time and the money, I wholeheartedly encourage you to give this game a try.

For those who come after.

This was really quite something.  A compelling cast of characters, exquisite story writing and dialogue, fun combat, and an unforgettable soundtrack.  Just a wonderful experience from beginning to end that manages to stay with you.  Entirely worth the praise it has been given by many.

Spiritually, it’s like NieR Automata. The way the story is told is that you don’t understand what’s happening, but because of how they act you’re convinced that it all matters. And you’re like: why are they talking so confidently about some nonsense? And so it goes until the end. And at the end things suddenly form a whole picture. Truly a diamond.
This may be....the greatest game of all time. Absolute masterpiece. Get it.

Very quickly became on of my favorite games of all time. The combat loop is engaging and can be challenging, especially as the game goes on. The music is so compelling and the story is very good. The characters are interesting as well and kept me engaged with them.
good game mechanics, very pretty, story is compelling, fighting is fun and complex without being stupid and difficult to remember.

Glad I was able to pick this up on sale, I enjoyed the story, soundtrack is phenomenal, and combat is fun (basically a rinse and repeat till you get the patterns of the enemy). 

Overall, would highly recommend getting it, sale or no sale. Has a good difficulty curve and isn't a pain in the ass (for most bosses at least). 9/10

Amazing story, characters and voice acting. 
Wish I could give myself amnesia to reexperience the story all again. 

I ain't the best as parrying and dodging, but everything is doable and love the fact that there are no difficulty locked achievements.
Fantastic story, music, characters and the world is so beautiful. I wish they went more in with the Writers in this story, but maybe in the sequel, I hope.
One of those games you wish you could play for the first time all over again - But even after finishing the story the side content is so good you just cannot put it down. 33/10

Simplesmente sensacional! Acho que nunca mais vou jogar nada parecido em termos de gráfico, música e história.

Achei a jogabilidade em si boa também. Não sou mais tão fã de jogos de combate por turno, mas ainda sim é bem dinâmico e fluido, um dos melhores do tipo que já joguei. Tudo que o Final Fantasy deveria ser hoje, mas não é... X-D

TLDR: 10/10 Worth a play even if you don't like turn based RPGs (like me). I recommend avoiding spoilers before you play this game. The experience is way better that way.


It took me around 26 hours to finish the game on Expeditioner (normal difficulty). You might be able to finish this in around 20 hours if you push through only the main story. I only got like 50% of the trophies


Story was well made. It gives you a lot to think about both during and after playing it. My favorite part was theory crafting what was going on. 

Music was phenomenal. 8 hours of music on the Spotify album. Need I say more?

Gameplay was satisfying and engaging. I never had any issue with the quick time events and each character have distinct gameplay styles that felt balanced (except Maelle, she is still a bit too strong)

Graphics were pretty bad for me. I feel like the pure amount of post processing effects weighed down the game. Cutscenes would stutter once every minute. The area around the hair looked like space and time were being stretch beyond their limits. There were a couple of times where I couldn't see what was happening during cutscenes because of the amount of stuff on screen. I will say that most of that I could have messed with, but I just stuck with turning off motion blur.

The best RPG I have played in decades, if you are on the fence, and don't want influenced by longer reviews, just take it with the grain of salt, that not since legend of the dragoon has a original story had this much motion.

Fantastic! I would recommend this game to anyone! I didn't even like turn based rpg's but had so much fun with this combat system. The story was amazing and the art direction might make this the most beautiful game I've ever played. I will say I didn't love the difficultly scaling in the post game content. I felt like I beat the story and I went to keep exploring and all of the sudden everything is one shotting and I'm dealing almost no damage. I'm sure if I kept exploring long enough I could have found stronger gear and pictos but I just wasn't as motivated since the story was already over and it felt like I was overstaying my welcome in the world. The story and uncovering the truth of the world was what Kept me so engaged for this game that when it was over I just lost that motivation. Still a 10/10 that I would recommend to anyone
So far, it's atmospheric, the combat is differentiated and engaging, and the story and voice acting are both top notch.  So far so good!
